text:
Your working directory is a checkout of the real react-hook-form repository at the commit where the bug below reproduces. Find and fix the bug in the library source.

Bug: when a form is created in the disabled state (`useForm({ disabled: true })`), calling `setValue(name, value, { shouldDirty: true })` fails to mark anything dirty. After registering a field and then programmatically setting a value that differs from its default with `shouldDirty: true`, `getFieldState(name).isDirty`, `formState.isDirty`, and `formState.dirtyFields` all incorrectly stay in their pristine state — even though the new value clearly differs from the default. Conversely, programmatically setting the field back to its default value on a disabled form should clear that dirty state again. The root cause is that the dirty-tracking path treats a disabled form as 'never track dirtiness' and swallows the update entirely. The correct behavior is that an explicit programmatic dirty request (`setValue` with `shouldDirty: true`) is honored even while the form is disabled, while ordinary user interaction (typing/blur) on a disabled form still does NOT track dirtiness. Fix the library source (under `src/`); do not just add workarounds in test files.

Environment notes: dependencies are already installed (via `npm install -g pnpm@10.34.5 && CYPRESS_INSTALL_BINARY=0 pnpm install --no-frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts`). Run tests with `node_modules/.bin/jest --config ./scripts/jest/jest.config.js src/__tests__/useForm/formState.test.tsx`.

message: `--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.
`--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.

text: I’ll trace the disabled-form dirty-state logic, patch the source path that distinguishes user interaction from explicit `setValue` requests, then run the targeted Jest suite.

The dirty helper currently wraps both dirty and touch handling in `if (!_options.disabled)`, so `setValue(..., { shouldDirty: true })` never reaches it. I’m checking nearby tests and call semantics to ensure the fix still suppresses dirty tracking from disabled user events while allowing only the explicit request.

input.command: /bin/bash -lc "rg -n \"disabled.*dirty|dirty.*disabled|shouldDirty\" src/__tests__/useForm src/__tests__ | head -120 && sed -n '180,290p' src/logic/createFormControl.ts && sed -n '960,1020p' src/logic/createFormControl.ts"
/bin/bash -lc "git diff -- src/logic/createFormControl.ts && sed -n '210,230p' src/logic/createFormControl.ts && sed -n '438,450p' src/logic/createFormControl.ts"
/bin/bash -lc "rg -n \"_getDirty\\(\" src/logic/createFormControl.ts src | head -80 && sed -n '144,165p' src/useForm.ts && sed -n '1260,1300p' src/logic/createFormControl.ts"
/bin/bash -lc 'git log --oneline -8 -- src/logic/createFormControl.ts && git log -S"shouldDirty" --oneline --all -- src/logic/createFormControl.ts | head -20'

text: The first source change enables the helper for `shouldDirty: true`, but one remaining guard in `_getDirty()` would let the field-level state update and then immediately force form-level `isDirty` back to false in `useForm`’s synchronization effect. I’m removing that global disabled short-circuit; the event path remains gated, so disabled typing/blur still cannot invoke dirty tracking.

text:
Fixed the disabled-form dirty tracking bug in [src/logic/createFormControl.ts](/home/user/workspace/src/logic/createFormControl.ts):

- `setValue(..., { shouldDirty: true })` now tracks dirtiness when disabled.
- Returning to the default clears dirty state.
- Disabled user interaction still does not track dirtiness.

Verified with:

`formState.test.tsx` and `setValue.test.tsx` — 90 tests passed.
